Based on the information provided, Phi Kappa Psi and other fraternities on the University of Virginia campus may have a difficult time suing Rolling Stone for defamation due to the nature of defamation law and the specific circumstances of the case. The university, as a governmental entity, cannot sue for defamation. A fraternity is considered a group, and it is not clear if the defamatory statements were about specific individuals within the group, making it challenging for the group or its members to bring a successful defamation lawsuit. However, if the fraternity can establish that the statements were about a comparatively small group of individuals and that the defamatory part is easily imputed against all members, an individual member may be able to sue. The success of any potential lawsuit would depend on various factors, including the ability to prove falsity, damages, and intent.

The author, who was the first woman and the first person of color to serve in the Kentucky State Senate in 1967, expresses her disappointment with Rand Paul's presidential campaign and his record on civil rights and voting rights legislation. She believes Paul's words of inclusion are hollow, as his policies do not align with the needs of minority communities. The author highlights Paul's past statements and actions, including his opposition to the Civil Rights Act, the Voting Rights Act, and the Department of Education, as evidence of his outdated views. She concludes by stating that the American people deserve a leader who respects their intelligence, does not pander, and works to build on the progress made over the last five decades, rather than dismantling it.

their significant age difference and the controversial beginning of their relationship. They married soon after her release in 2005 and now have two teenage daughters. Mary Kay is currently 53, and Vili is 31.

The case was set for trial next week but has been dismissed. West pleaded no contest to a misdemeanor count of battery in the incident last year and was sentenced to probation, anger management sessions, and community service. West has also previously settled a separate assault allegation from a man at a Beverly Hills chiropractor's office in 2014. Allred issued a statement saying, "Celebrities are not above the law, and they have no right to physically attack someone simply because they were asked a question."

The cargo hold of Alaska Airlines Flight 448 experienced unusual banging, leading to an emergency landing due to a ramp agent who had fallen asleep during his shift. The cargo hold is pressurized and temperature controlled, ensuring the man's safety

Passengers heard the banging and believed there was a problem with the landing gear. A federal air marshal intervened, and the man was taken to a hospital as a precaution. The employee was scheduled to finish his shift before the flight, but was missing during the pre-departure huddle. Alaska Airlines is investigating the incident.

The Fourteenth Amendment grants citizenship to those born in the United States and subject to its jurisdiction.

Afalava, an American Samoan, has been denied the right to vote due to the federal government's insistence that he is not a citizen. This situation is a remnant of past discriminatory practices, such as the Dred Scott case, which denied citizenship to free African-Americans. Another example is the case of Isabel Gonzalez, a Puerto Rican woman who was prevented from entering the mainland and becoming a citizen due to the Supreme Court's belief in the racial inferiority of tropical peoples. These cases, rooted in discrimination and fear, have been repudiated by the American people and the Constitution. It is long overdue for American Samoans,
Afalava, to be granted the full citizenship rights that have been afforded to all other U.S. territories' inhabitants.

Senate passed a Medicare reform bill on Tuesday with a bipartisan vote of 92 to 8, including a permanent solution to the "doc fix" and a two-year extension of the Children's Health Insurance Program. The House had already approved the same bill, and President Barack Obama is expected to sign it. The bill aims to retire the outdated Medicare reimbursement system, but some senators, including Ted Cruz and Marco Rubio, expressed concerns about the more than $200 billion price tag and voted against it. The House Speaker John Boehner urged the Senate to pass the House-passed bill without delay to prevent significant cuts to physicians' payments.

The trailer was set to premiere in Imax theaters on Monday, March 25, 2016. Ben Affleck stars as Batman and Henry Cavill as Superman in the film, which also features Amy Adams. The cost of obtaining Mark Hamill's autograph at "Star Wars" celebration is not mentioned in the provided article.

The Pope, as the spiritual leader of the Catholic Church and the head of the Vatican state, faces the challenge of balancing moral and political concerns. This was evident on Sunday when Pope Francis commemorated the centennial of the Armenian genocide. Previous popes have avoided labeling the killings as genocide, but Francis quoted John Paul II's description of it as the "first genocide of the 20th century." This decision maintained the Vatican's concern for continuity while acknowledging the historical facts. However, Turkey, which disputes the genocide label, recalled its ambassador and summoned the Vatican's ambassador for consultations in response.

The second robotic probe sent into the Fukushima nuclear plant captured images of a strange green glow. TEPCO reported that the robot detected lower radiation levels and temperature than expected, indicating effective cooling systems. Akira Ono, the chief of Fukushima Daiichi plant, stated that this was a significant step forward in the decommissioning process. The yellow discoloration in the images was suggested to be a grating, but the cause was unknown. The green glow could not be seen from other angles. The first robot, which stalled after moving about 10 meters, had already collected data from 14 of the 18 targeted locations before being cut off. The second robot successfully collected data from all 11 points as scheduled. The high radiation levels inside the three damaged reactors remain unsafe for workers to enter, and decommissioning work is estimated to cost $50 billion and take years to complete. TEPCO considered the robotic probe an "unprecedented" experiment.

The plane, a Cessna 414, was coming back from the NCAA Final Four championship game in Indianapolis. Ward and Leetch were well-respected and much-loved colleagues in the Illinois State athletics department. Ward, a standout player at the University of Alabama at Birmingham, had a coaching career that included stops at Jacksonville State University, the University of Mississippi, and China before coming to Illinois State. Leetch joined ISU in 2005 and had various roles before leaving to serve as director of athletics at Whitworth University in Spokane, Washington, and returning to Illinois State in 2013. her ex-fiance, Nick Loeb, revolves around two frozen embryos they created through in vitro fertilization in 2013

Loeb is suing Vergara in Los Angeles to prevent her from destroying the embryos, which are currently being held at the Art Reproductive Center in Beverly Hills. The lawsuit, filed anonymously to protect their privacy, alleges that the couple's plan was to eventually have children together. However, they had already used two surrogate mothers without success. Loeb wants to implant the embryos in a surrogate and bring them to term, but he doesn't want any financial obligations from Vergara. The case has raised questions about who has the right to embryos and when life begins. Fertility specialist Dr. David Tourgeman explained that typically, the mother or the origin of the egg is given power of attorney over the embryos. If there's a disagreement, the courts usually get involved to decide who legally owns the embryos. The documents signed during the fertility process will only apply in the case of death of one of the partners. California attorney Vance Owen added that this lawsuit is bringing up interesting issues, including religious, moral, legal, and contract issues. The case is currently a gray area of law and will likely be decided by a California court

Sissa Abu Dahou

a 65-year-old grandmother in Luxor, Egypt, has been dressing as a man for 43 years to provide for her family and avoid oppression in the conservative Muslim state. She started this tradition after her husband's death when she was only 21 and pregnant with her daughter, Houda. Dahou worked various jobs, including as a brick maker and a shoeshiner, to support her family. Her determination allowed Houda to receive an education and eventually marry and have children of her own. Dahou's hard work and perseverance earned her respect as a man in her community, but she recently gained praise as a woman when Egypt's President, Abdel Fattah el-Sisi, crowned her as one of Egypt's ideal mothers in an official ceremony.

Dahou remains proud of her decision and continues to wear male attire. "If I was really a man, I would not have done this," she told her daughter. "I would have gotten remarried. I would have left you who knows where. You would have been treated without dignity or left homeless. Thank God I was able to protect you."

Despite the boat being in bad shape and listing to one side, Jordan was in surprisingly good condition. He had left Conway, South Carolina, to fish in the ocean but encountered a storm that capsized his boat and broke his mast. Unable to fix it right away due to a broken shoulder, he managed to rig a makeshift mast and sail, but progress was slow due to the currents. Jordan endured numerous challenges during his time at sea, including collecting fresh water and finding food. He was eventually rescued on Thursday evening and taken to Sentara Norfolk General Hospital. Coast Guard officials have confirmed Jordan's story, and his father, Frank Jordan, expressed relief and amazement at his son's condition upon their reunion. Jordan is an unemployed truck driver who lived on his boat at a marina in Conway. His father had reported him and his boat missing on January 29. The French prosecutor, Brice Robin, stated that no video footage from on board Germanwings Flight 9525 has been used in the investigation. Reports of a cell phone video showing the final moments of the flight are not confirmed by the investigators or the French accident investigation agency, BEA. Co-pilot Andreas Lubitz had a history of depression, which was known to Lufthansa, the parent company of Germanwings, but he was still allowed to continue training and fly. The motive for Lubitz's alleged actions remains under investigation, with theories suggesting he may have feared losing his pilot's license due to his medical condition. No human remains were visible at the crash site, but recovery teams will continue searching for them. French President Francois Hollande expects all victims to be identified using DNA analysis by the end of the week. The recovery of personal belongings, including cell phones, will begin on Wednesday.

Burns left the band before its third studio album, "Nuthin Fancy," in 1975, due to exhaustion from touring. He was not involved in the 1977 plane crash that claimed the lives of three band members, including Van Zant. Lynyrd Skynyrd was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2006, and Gary Rossington remains the only original member still in the band. The nuclear submarine that is currently undergoing repairs at the Zvyozdochka shipyard in Russia has caught on fire. The fire started due to welding work and is located between the submarine's light and pressure hull, specifically in the rubber insulation. The submarine is in a dry dock and there is no ammunition, armaments, chemically active substances, or fissionable materials on board. The nuclear fuel from the reactor has been unloaded, and no one has been injured. The personnel have evacuated the premises, and the fire does not pose a threat to people or the shipyard.

The California Public Utilities Commission has ordered Pacific Gas & Electric (PG&E) to pay a record $1.6 billion penalty for unsafe operation of its gas transmission system, following the 2010 pipeline rupture in San Bruno that killed eight people and injured over 50 others. Of the total penalty, $850 million will be allocated for gas transmission pipeline safety infrastructure improvements, and an additional $50 million for other remedies to enhance pipeline safety. PG&E CEO, Tony Earley, expressed deep regret for the tragedy and the company's commitment to becoming the safest energy company in the US. The CPUC President, Michael Picker, acknowledged the failure to uphold public trust and prevent the loss of lives and destruction of homes. PG&E has already paid over $500 million in claims to San Bruno victims and families, and has replaced over 800 miles of pipe, installed new gas leak technology, and implemented nine of the National Transportation Safety Board's recommendations. The previous record penalty was a $146 million penalty against Southern California Edison Company in 2008 for falsifying customer and worker safety data.

Nelly, the hip-hop artist from St. Louis, was arrested on drug charges in Tennessee after a state trooper stopped his bus for not having the required U.S. Department of Transportation and International Fuel Tax Association stickers. During the subsequent search of the bus, the trooper found methamphetamine, marijuana, drug paraphernalia, and several handguns, including a gold-plated .50-caliber Desert Eagle pistol, a .45-caliber Taurus pistol, and a .500 Smith & Wesson magnum. Nelly, whose real name is Cornell Haynes, was charged with felony possession of drugs, simple possession of marijuana, and possession of drug paraphernalia. He was taken to the Putnam County Jail and later posted bond before leaving. The other passenger was also arrested. The incident occurred on Interstate 40 in Putnam County on Saturday. Nelly's representatives couldn't be reached for comment.

John Travolta, a high-profile Scientologist, has expressed his disinterest in HBO's documentary "Going Clear: Scientology and the Prison of Belief," stating that he hasn't seen it and doesn't plan to, as he's had positive experiences with the Church of Scientology for the past 40 years and doesn't have anything negative to add. Travolta is currently premiering his new film, "The Forger," in Clearwater, Florida, where the documentary also explores the organization's ties to the showbiz industry. The actor believes the documentary is based on disgruntled individuals' experiences and doesn't want to communicate anything that isn't true for him or Tom Cruise. Travolta credits Scientology for helping him cope with the death of his son, Jett, in 2009.

The Exotic Driving Experience at Walt Disney World Speedway experienced a tragic accident on Sunday when a Lamborghini crashed into a guardrail, resulting in the death of the passenger, 36-year-old Gary Terry, and minor injuries to the driver, 24-year-old Tavon Watson. Petty Holdings, the company operating the event, expressed their deepest sympathies to those involved in the accident.

95 passengers and five crew members on the Celebrity Infinity cruise ship have fallen ill with symptoms of gastrointestinal illness, including vomiting and diarrhea. A total of 100 people have been affected out of the ship's 2,117 passengers and 964 crew members. The CDC is investigating the cause of the outbreak and plans to meet the ship in San Diego on Monday.

Celebrity Infinity, which left San Diego on March 29 and last stopped in Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, on April 10, has experienced similar outbreaks in 2006 and 2013. The ship, built in 2001 and refurbished in 2011, has been undergoing increased cleaning and disinfection procedures, as well as keeping passengers informed and collecting specimens for CDC testing.

Tomasz Lazar is a Polish photographer who has spent years documenting the stories of adults who were forcibly deported from Poland to Siberia as children during World War II. Through his photographs and interviews, Lazar has captured the faces and stories of a generation that was stolen from Poland by the Soviet authorities. Many of these survivors experienced great trauma, including the loss of family members, and their stories were suppressed until the fall of the Soviet Union. The presence of a blood moon around Easter is not a coincidence, as both holidays are connected to the full moon.

Xanax, or alprazolam, is a benzodiazepine medication commonly used to alleviate symptoms of anxiety. It works by enhancing the effects of the neurotransmitter GABA in the brain, leading to a calming effect. Xanax is known for its rapid onset of action, making it effective for acute anxiety episodes. However, long-term use of Xanax can lead to tolerance development, requiring higher doses for the same effect and increasing the risk of dependence and withdrawal symptoms. For chronic anxiety management, other treatment options like therapy, lifestyle modifications, or alternative medications may be more sustainable.
A holistic approach that incorporates various strategies is often the key to long-term well-being in managing anxiety.

Wahl was born Sara Rosalie Erwin in 1924 in Gordon, Kansas. Raised by her grandmother after the loss of her mother and other family members, Wahl developed a deep understanding of the struggles faced by the less fortunate. She studied sociology at the University of Kansas during World War II, where her passion for social justice was ignited.

After marrying Ross Wahl and relocating to Minnesota, Wahl spent several years as a homemaker and community volunteer before making a bold decision to attend William Mitchell College of Law in 1962. Despite being a mother of four, she excelled in her studies and later worked as an assistant public defender, building a strong reputation in criminal defense.

Her experience and dedication caught the attention of Governor Rudy Perpich, who appointed Wahl as the first female justice in the Minnesota Supreme Court in 1977. During her tenure, she chaired task forces on gender fairness and racial bias, leading to significant reforms that transformed the treatment of women and minorities within the court system.

Nationally recognized for her work with the American Bar Association, she played a pivotal role in reshaping legal education and accreditation standards. Her contributions focused on preparing future lawyers for real-world practice by emphasizing skills training.

The California drug kingpin, Luis Alfredo Jacobo, has been sentenced to four life terms in federal prison for directing methamphetamine conspiracies in Oklahoma and Missouri. He was also given an additional four-year sentence on 21 counts of unlawful use of a communication facility

Jacobo orchestrated these operations from Bakersfield, California, and received bulk shipments of meth, which he oversaw the distribution of, controlling pricing, methods of delivery, and payments. Three distinct drug conspiracies were operated by Jacobo's regional managers in Oklahoma, and evidence presented during the trial included communications, money exchanges, and the discovery of 231 pounds of methamphetamine and over $465,000 in a storage unit in Grove, Oklahoma. Jacobo's conviction was the result of Operation "Pullin Chains," a multi-agency effort led by the U.S. Attorney's Office and the Drug Enforcement Administration to target high-level drug traffickers and criminal organizations.

The Midwest and Northern Plains experienced unexpected Northern Lights displays on the nights of October 20th and 21st, 2023, following a weak Coronal Mass Ejection (CME). States such as Minnesota, Michigan, and even southern Wisconsin reported sightings, along with North Dakota and Omaha, Nebraska. Despite the CME's slower solar wind speeds and weaker projection, it resulted in a noticeable Aurora display due to the alignment of solar energy with Earth's magnetic field. For more information on the Northern Lights and forecasting techniques, check out Charlie Bourdo's "How to" video.

Cristina Michelle Husbands, a 48-year-old woman from Dallas, Texas, was sentenced to 64 months in prison for her role in a bank fraud conspiracy that led to the theft of hundreds of thousands of dollars from banks across multiple states. She was ordered to pay $481,140 in restitution and will serve five years of supervised release after her prison term

Husbands pled guilty to stealing $108,000 from Regions Bank customer accounts in Mississippi, but her attorney emphasized her vulnerability and desperation during the time she was homeless, struggling with drug addiction, and in an abusive relationship. She was targeted by recruiters at homeless shelters and clinics for opiate addiction, who used her to carry out fraudulent activities, including creating false identification documents to access and withdraw funds. The scheme involved at least four known victims and various amounts of money withdrawn in cashier's checks and cash from Regions Bank branches between November 2021 and April 2022. Husbands was already incarcerated in Texas on charges including fraud, forgery, and identity theft when she was arrested federally in Mississippi. Assistant U.S. Attorney John Meynardie prosecuted the case.

Richie's "King of Hearts" residency at Encore Theater at Wynn Las Vegas will continue from June 26 to November 16, 2024. If your door lock or latch is not working properly, you can try the following do-it-yourself repair techniques before replacing the entire lockset. If the door won't lock, try tapping the end of a chisel under the door stop on the striker side of the jamb to pry it up and remove it. After removing the door stop, align it back on the jamb and nail it back in place, setting it back 1/4 inch from its original location. If necessary, you may also need to enlarge the latch hole in the jamb and move the strike plate to ensure proper alignment between the latch and the strike plate hole. Make sure all hinge screws are tight before attempting to adjust the strike plate.

Step 8: Sew a 1/4-inch seam along the binding edge, starting at one corner and sewing toward the next. Be sure to backstitch at the beginning and end of each seam.

To set Mozilla Firefox as your default web browser in Windows 7

1. Open the Mozilla Firefox browser.
2. Click on the Firefox menu button, represented by three horizontal lines, located in the upper right hand corner of the browser window.
3. When the pop-out menu appears, select "Options."

Step 1: In the Firefox Options window, click on the "General" tab.
Step 2: Scroll down to the "Startup" section and click the "Make Default" button next to "Make Default web browser."
Step 3: A User Account Control prompt will appear. Click "Yes" to confirm the change.
Step 4: Close the Firefox Options window.

Now, any hyperlink that is selected in Windows 7 will cause Firefox to open and the address of the link will be retrieved.

To see red blood cells, follow these steps to make a blood smear:

1. Place a clean glass slide on a flat surface and add one small drop of blood to one end.
2. Take another clean slide and hold it at an angle of about 45 degrees. Touch the blood with one end of the slide so the blood runs along the edge by capillary action.
3. Push the slide carefully along the length of the first slide to produce a thin smear of blood.
4. Repeat this process to make two smears. Allow them to air dry and label them clearly.
5. Once dried, place the smears in the provided slide transport containers.

Remember, blood can carry diseases, so always take precautions to avoid coming into contact with another person's blood. If handling someone else's blood is unavoidable, wear rubber gloves. Sterilize the needle before use and be sure to follow all safety precautions.

To transfer audiobooks from OverDrive for Windows
an iPhone, iPad, or iPod using iTunes, follow these steps

1. In iTunes, turn on the "Manually manage music" setting.
2. Download the audiobook you want from OverDrive in the Audiobooks section of iTunes.
3. Connect your Apple device to your computer using a USB cable.
4. In iTunes, select your device under "Devices" in the left column.
5. Choose the "Books" tab above the main iTunes window.
6. Select the audiobook(s) you want to transfer and click "Sync Audiobooks."
7. Click "Apply" to finish the syncing process.
Alternatively, you can use Syncios Audiobook Transfer to transfer audiobooks between your computer and your iOS device.
